summary: TASK 5 ground truth established from canonical Microsoft sources + repo's own empirical spikes. (a) CONFIRMED: the classic VS WinForms designer never executes the designed form's constructor or InitializeComponent — it instantiates the form's BASE class as the root designer surface, parses InitializeComponent via the language CodeDomProvider into CodeDOM, and replays it through CodeDomSerializers onto live child controls instantiated from their COMPILED types (canonical source: MS archived blog 'How does the Windows Forms designer in Visual Studio load a Form?'). Consequences: most-derived ctor/Load code never runs; inherited forms DO run the base's ctor+InitializeComponent (base must be compiled); a form inheriting an ABSTRACT base fails; hand-edited InitializeComponent breaks parsing. (b) CONFIRMED: for .NET (net6+) projects VS runs the designer OUT-OF-PROCESS in DesignToolsServer.exe, which runs the project's exact .NET version and bitness; real controls live in the server, .NET-Framework ObjectProxy objects live in devenv, JSON-RPC in between, rendering projected onto the VS surface — the direct industry precedent for this repo's net9+net48 two-engine split. Vendor designers must be rebuilt against the VS-only-licensed Microsoft.WinForms.Designer.SDK; custom UITypeEditors need a 5-project client/server/protocol split. (c) DevExpress at design time: .NET-Fx path = installed design-time assemblies + AnyCPU constraint (32-bit devenv) + licenses.licx/LC pipeline (design-time-only license check); .NET path = DevExpress.Win.Design NuGet for the OOP designer. The repo's S5 spike + production engine-net48 empirically prove what DevExpress needs OUTSIDE VS: assembly probing against the app's bin, replicated app.config binding redirects, a real off-screen-visible realized HWND + message pump for async skin/layout, ISupportInitialize Begin/EndInit, LicenseException handling, and no ShadowCopy (delay-signed vendor dlls). (d) Fidelity gaps enumerated: interpretation surfaces (net9 engine) fail-closed on BinaryFormatter resx/ToolStrip serialization/unrepresentable statements; compiled-instantiation surfaces (net48 engine) run MORE code than VS (most-derived ctor side effects, abstract roots fail, DesignMode=false runtime paths) — the two engines bracket VS's behavior from opposite sides.
keyFacts: (a) CONFIRMED — canonical source (Raghavendra Prabhu, MS, archived: learn.microsoft.com/en-us/archive/blogs/rprabhu/how-does-the-windows-forms-designer-in-visual-studio-load-a-form): 'the designer is not really instantiating Form1 at all. It is creating an instance of the base class of Form1' and 'Form1's constructor and InitializeComponent are never really invoked. Instead, the designer parses the statements in InitializeComponent'. Parsing = language CodeDomProvider parser -> CodeDOM -> CodeDomSerializers deserialize into real Components added to the design-time root. Loader stack: CodeDomDesignerLoader (public abstract, CodeDOM-based designer loader) over BasicDesignerLoader; VS's concrete loader is internal.

(a) Consequences, all from the same canonical source: (1) controls added programmatically in the ctor or Load handler do NOT appear at design time; (2) a breakpoint in the designed form's own InitializeComponent never hits; (3) INHERITED forms: designing Form2:Form1 instantiates the COMPILED Form1 — so Form1's ctor AND InitializeComponent DO execute at design time (breakpoints there DO hit; base-class side effects like DB connects fire in the designer — confirmed in the post's comment thread); (4) an abstract form can itself be designed (its base is instantiated), but a form INHERITING an abstract base fails ('cannot create an instance... type is declared abstract'); (5) InitializeComponent is only guaranteed parseable for what the designer itself serialized — arbitrary hand edits break loading.

(a) Child controls ARE instantiated from their compiled types — their ctors and OnPaint run at design time (that's why the project must be built for custom controls to render, and why bad control ctors crash/hang devenv). Design-time detection by control authors is famously unreliable: Component.DesignMode is false inside the ctor and for nested controls (no ISite yet); LicenseManager.UsageMode is not reliably Designtime during construction; and in the modern OOP designer LicenseManager.UsageMode reports RUNTIME (dotnet/winforms#10960) — so vendor code gated on these behaves differently across designers and any third-party host.

(a) CAUTION for the plan: a Microsoft Q&A answer claims the designer 'compiles a shadow assembly and executes InitializeComponent' — this contradicts the canonical archived MS source and the observable facts (breakpoints don't hit; ctor code doesn't run). Treat the parse/deserialize model as ground truth. VS 17.5+ OOP designer replaced CodeDOM-based design-time serialization with Roslyn-based code generation (devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/winforms-codegen-update/), but the 'parse, never execute, base-class root' model is unchanged.

(b) CONFIRMED — Microsoft Learn (learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/desktop/winforms/controls-design/designer-differences-framework): the .NET designer is OUT-OF-PROCESS: 'DesignToolsServer.exe... runs on the same version and platform of .NET that your app has been set up to target, such as .NET 9 and x64'. Real components live in DesignToolsServer; '.NET Framework proxy objects are created for each component and control' inside devenv; the Designer SDK transfers WinForms-typical data via JSON-RPC (devblogs 'State of the Windows Forms Designer for .NET Applications'); server renders the form and projects it onto the VS client surface. Motivation: .NET and .NET Fx cannot share a process, and CodeDom serialization must run on the project's runtime for fidelity. This is the exact industry precedent for the repo's two-engine (net9 DesignSurface + net48 AppDomain) split.

(b) Vendor extensibility under the OOP designer: control designers MUST be recoded against Microsoft.WinForms.Designer.SDK — ControlDesigner/ComponentTray moved to Microsoft.DotNet.DesignTools.Designers, action lists to ...Designers.Actions, behaviors/snaplines to ...Designers.Behaviors; legacy System.Windows.Forms.Design designers do not load. Custom UITypeEditors require a 5-project split (Control / Control.Client [.NET Fx UI in devenv] / Control.Server [.NET designer logic] / Control.Protocol / Control.Package NuGet). The SDK's EULA restricts use to the Visual Studio product family (repo plan §0.3.11) and the DesignToolsServer pipe protocol is undocumented — rehosting Microsoft's engine is a confirmed legal+technical non-starter (plan §3, codex-verified).

(b) Even Microsoft's own OOP designer has compat gaps worth citing as precedent for honest degraded tiers: a transitive WPF PackageReference can break it (dotnet/winforms#10804); 32-bit .NET Framework projects got a separate 'designer selection' fallback (VS devblogs 'WinForms Designer Selection for 32-bit .NET Framework Projects'); LicenseManager-using third-party controls crashed the SDK-style-project designer with E_NOTIMPL because licenses.licx/LC is a .NET-Fx-era pipeline (dotnet/project-system#4938).

(c) DevExpress on .NET Framework at design time (docs.devexpress.com/WindowsForms/405547 troubleshoot-design-time): design-time functionality comes from installed DevExpress design-time assemblies (DXperience installer registers toolbox + *.Design assemblies); devenv is 32-bit (through VS2019 era) and 'has to load all referenced assemblies at design time' — an x64-only PlatformTarget breaks the designer (official fix: temporarily AnyCPU); stale licenses.licx entries referencing outdated strongly-named DevExpress versions are the classic design-time breakage (official fix: delete licenses.licx + bin/obj, restart VS).

(c) licenses.licx mechanics (DevExpress CTO blog 'Licenses.licx file woes', 2009 + support tickets): VS records every licensed control used at design time into licenses.licx; at build, lc.exe loads each listed assembly, runs its license code, and embeds a .licenses resource into the executable. The check is DESIGN-TIME only — runtime redistribution is royalty-free. UNCERTAIN: whether current DevExpress versions (v24/v25) still emit/require licenses.licx for WinForms — no removal announcement found; the repo's S5 spike explicitly checks for embedded .licenses resources and classifies LicenseException (exit code 3) as a distinct failure mode, and it did NOT fire for the user's vendor fork (PGMUI).

(c) DevExpress on .NET 6+ at design time: a single NuGet package DevExpress.Win.Design supplies OOP-designer design-time support for the whole WinForms suite (docs.devexpress.com/WindowsForms/405090 'Create a DevExpress-powered .NET App with Design-Time Support'); DevExpress notes its size affects form load time in the designer; known breakages include DevExpress WPF transitive references killing the OOP designer. These SDK-based design-time assemblies are exactly what a raw DesignSurface host CANNOT load (plan risk R34) — hence repo's degraded model: real runtime render + generic grid + reflectively-read DECLARED smart-tag menus (engine-net48/VendorSmartTags.cs) instead of hosting DevExpress DesignerActionLists.

(c) EMPIRICAL ground truth (repo spike S5 + production engine-net48, proven pixel-perfect on a real DevExpress-fork XtraUserControl): instantiating the COMPILED control type outside VS renders correctly IF: (1) AssemblyResolve probing covers the app's bin + vendor component dirs (S5 Program.cs OnResolve); (2) the child AppDomain gets the app's own config + synthesized binding redirects so 'the DevExpress graph resolves as at runtime' (DomainManager.cs:9-16, ChildDomainConfig.cs — a class-library target has no config and previously broke); (3) the control tree gets a REAL realized HWND: off-screen-visible window at -20000 (never hidden/minimized — S4 matrix proved hidden=blank), Form.Show(), then ~20 message-pump iterations 'so DevExpress async layout / skin application settles' (S5 Program.cs:131, RenderWorker.cs:1322); (4) a Form/XtraForm root is hosted DIRECTLY (top-level control cannot be added to a control — RenderWorker.cs:1288); (5) ShadowCopyFiles OFF because it breaks delay-signed vendor assemblies — consequence: user dlls are pinned in place (MSB3027) requiring an explicit domain-release protocol (DomainManager.cs); (6) a 25s kill-gate for hanging vendor ctors (S5 Program.cs:77-81).

(c) ISupportInitialize: the VS serializer brackets every component implementing it with BeginInit/EndInit in InitializeComponent; DevExpress grids/editors depend on this batching. Any parse-and-replay engine must preserve and replay these calls — the repo shipped 'ISupportInitialize round-trip' in 0.12.0 (commit 72274ac). DevExpress skinning (LookAndFeel) is applied by the control's own runtime code, so the compiled-instantiation path reproduces skins automatically (S5 proved pixel parity); UNCERTAIN: exact modern VS design-time skin-selection mechanics (DefaultLookAndFeel component / project settings) — not verified.

(d) Fidelity-gap taxonomy. VS's OWN documented gaps (inherent to parse-don't-execute): ctor/Load/override code invisible; DesignMode-guarded branches; runtime data absent; abstract-inheritance failures; DPI mismatch (classic designer renders at devenv's DPI — the yellow 'restart at 100% scaling' bar). Interpretation-based surfaces (repo net9 engine) ADD: unrepresentable-statement read-only fallbacks (empirical gap-map in FINDINGS.md: ToolStrip CodeDom serialization crashes on .NET 9 BinaryFormatter removal; ImageList ImageStream/binary resx = PlatformNotSupportedException; extenders/resx interpret-only), default-value over-emission requiring explicit-echo normalization (S2a finding #1), TypeConverter/default divergence across runtimes (plan §0.3.6 hard gate). Compiled-instantiation surfaces (repo net48 engine) run MORE code than VS: most-derived ctor side effects execute (VS only runs BASE ctors), abstract root types fail entirely (VS classic can design an abstract form itself), DesignMode=false so controls take RUNTIME paths (timers, animations, data access) — mitigated by timeout kill-gate + live-edit gating to Control||ToolStripItem (Timer.Start incident, handoff 2026-07-09).

(d) Capture-layer gaps compound interpretation gaps: Control.DrawToBitmap has documented holes (ActiveX not painted, RichTextBox border-only, reverse z-order for container children — plan §4.3); S4 matrix proved hidden/minimized windows capture blank and only visible-off-screen windows capture fully; WGC on fully-offscreen windows delivers only a still frame (S4b, parked). Any 'ground-truth render' claim must therefore be scoped to realized, visible-offscreen HWNDs.

(repo) The two-engine architecture maps onto VS's own split: net9 engine = VS-classic-like parse/interpret surface (DesignSurface + Roslyn splice, byte-minimal targeted edits, fail-closed §6.5 gates); net48 engine = DesignToolsServer-like 'run the project's runtime' surface (child AppDomain per bin dir, compiled-type instantiation, live mutation, reflection-only DevExpress interop — no compile-time DevExpress reference, RenderWorker.cs:921). Key deliberate divergence from VS: net48 instantiates the MOST-DERIVED compiled type (VS instantiates the base) — trading VS's side-effect safety for DevExpress skin/pixel fidelity that VS itself only achieves via vendor SDK packages.

files.role: Aspirational v3 plan; §0 codex reconciliation is the authoritative layer: R34 vendor-SDK mismatch, DesignToolsServer non-starter (§3), Microsoft.WinForms.Designer.SDK EULA analysis (§0.3.11), cross-runtime hard gate (§0.3.6)
Pragmatic delta plan (2026-07-03, codex+workflow reconciled); honest current-state inventory + T0–T4 tiers; documents net48 raison d'etre = DevExpress LOB forms
Raw dual gap analysis feeding the parity plan (evidence file:line citations)

Phase-0/1 empirical spike results: S1 DesignSurface hosting, S2 round-trip zero-diff, S3 capture latency, S4 offscreen-capture matrix (hidden=blank), S4b WGC parked, gap-map of real-form constructs incl. BinaryFormatter/.NET-9 limits

S5 DevExpress de-risk spike: compiled-type instantiation outside VS; LicenseException classification (exit 3), embedded .licenses check, off-screen realized HWND + message pump, 25s ctor kill-gate, AssemblyResolve probing incl. vendor component dir

Production net48 host: one child AppDomain per user-project bin dir; app config + binding redirects so 'the DevExpress graph resolves as at runtime'; ShadowCopy OFF (delay-signed vendor dlls) -> dll pinning + release protocol + leaked-domain recycling
Synthesizes the child domain's ConfigurationFile (app's config + needed binding redirects; class-library targets have none of their own)

Real compiled-control instantiation + render: Form/XtraForm root hosted directly (line ~1288, 'Top-level control cannot be added'), off-screen Show + DoEvents pump for DevExpress skinning (~1304-1325), reflection-only DevExpress interop (XtraTabControl hit-testing/pages, TreeList Nodes), live mutation
Reads a vendor control's DECLARED smart-tag menu (DevExpress 'Tasks') off the compiled type — the degraded substitute for hosting SDK-based DesignerActionLists
Pure-Roslyn parse of a compiled control's .Designer.cs (no instantiation) — works for DevExpress/any vendor type
TypeDescriptor-based describe over live compiled instances (Control + IComponent/ToolStripItem) for the net48 property grid
Durable subsystem map: on-canvas ToolStrip pipeline

risks: UNCERTAIN: whether current DevExpress WinForms versions (v24/v25) still emit/require licenses.licx — no removal announcement found; older docs treat stale licenses.licx as the #1 design-time breakage. The S5 LicenseException gate exists but the empirical corpus is a single vendor fork (PGMUI) that did NOT license-fail; genuine licensed DevExpress installs may still throw LicenseException on bare Activator.CreateInstance, with no remediation in-engine.

LicenseProvider-gated vendor controls are unstable in ANY non-classic host: the OOP designer itself reports LicenseManager.UsageMode=Runtime (dotnet/winforms#10960) and SDK-style projects crashed on LC (dotnet/project-system#4938) — vendor license code paths are untested territory for both repo engines.

Design-mode detection divergence: repo engines site no components under a designer host on the net48 path, so DesignMode=false and controls take RUNTIME code paths (timers, data access, animations) that VS's designer suppresses — the Timer.Start live-edit incident (handoff 2026-07-09) is the proven instance; the general class of 'runtime-path side effects' remains open and only bounded by the kill-gate/process recycle.

Most-derived vs base instantiation: net48 engine runs the user form's own ctor (VS never does). Consequences: abstract root types fail where classic VS succeeds; ctor side effects (DB connects, file I/O) execute on open; conversely inherited-base fidelity is BETTER than an interpreter. This asymmetry should be a documented product boundary, not discovered by users.

SDK-based vendor design-time (DevExpress.Win.Design, smart tags, collection editors, custom UITypeEditors) is legally (VS-only EULA) and technically unhostable — vendor forms are permanently 'render + generic grid + declared-tasks readout', never full VS parity (plan R34). Marketing must not claim VS parity for vendor designers.

Web-sourced correction to keep out of the plan: the Microsoft Q&A claim that the designer 'creates a shadow assembly and executes InitializeComponent' contradicts the canonical archived MS source (parse/deserialize, never execute) — do not let that model leak into architecture docs.
x86-only vendor/COM dependencies require an x86 engine variant (bitness matrix, plan §8.2) — currently only x64 paths are exercised.
portBlockers: Microsoft.WinForms.Designer.SDK EULA restricts use to the Visual Studio product family — cannot rehost DesignToolsServer.exe or load DevExpress.Win.Design/SDK-based vendor designers in the VS Code extension (confirmed non-starter, plan §3/§0.3.11).

BinaryFormatter removal in .NET 9: ToolStrip/MenuStrip CodeDom whole-file serialization throws PlatformNotSupportedException and binary resx payloads (ImageList.ImageStream) cannot be regenerated on the net9 engine — targeted text edits work, full regenerate is permanently read-only for these constructs (FINDINGS.md gap-map).

licenses.licx / lc.exe pipeline is not implemented in either engine — a genuinely license-enforcing vendor control that demands a design-time license context has no in-product remediation (fail-closed + 'open in VS' is the only honest fallback).

Abstract root types: net48 compiled-type instantiation cannot design an abstract form/usercontrol at all (VS classic can, by instantiating its base) — parity gap inherent to the chosen fidelity model.

Hidden/minimized-window capture is physically blank (S4) and WGC on fully-offscreen windows yields only a still frame (S4b) — all rendering must go through visible-off-screen realized HWNDs; environments that forbid that (session 0, some RDP configs) are unverified.

ShadowCopy must stay OFF for delay-signed vendor assemblies, so open net48 designers pin user dlls (MSB3027 on rebuild) — mitigated by the release/recycle protocol but an inherent UX tax of in-place AppDomain loading.
